items.q: Why are DNA's two strands described as antiparallel?
items.correct: The strands run in opposite directions, one 5-prime to 3-prime and the other 3-prime to 5-prime, which is required for base pairing and replication.
items.misconception: Antiparallel just means the two strands are the same length.
items.random_wrong: Antiparallel means the strands repel each other.
items.why: Students translate 'antiparallel' loosely instead of as opposite 5-prime to 3-prime orientation.

items.q: In semi-conservative replication, what does each new DNA molecule contain?
items.correct: Each new molecule has one original template strand and one newly synthesised strand.
items.misconception: Each new DNA molecule is made entirely of brand-new strands.
items.random_wrong: The original DNA stays intact while a fully separate copy is built.
items.why: Students picture conservative replication rather than the semi-conservative model Meselson and Stahl demonstrated.

items.q: How many hydrogen bonds hold guanine and cytosine together?
items.correct: Guanine and cytosine pair with three hydrogen bonds, while adenine and thymine pair with two.
items.misconception: All base pairs are held by the same number of hydrogen bonds.
items.random_wrong: Guanine pairs with adenine using three hydrogen bonds.
items.why: Students forget that G-C pairs are stronger (three bonds) than A-T pairs (two bonds).

items.q: In DNA, adenine forms hydrogen bonds with:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: thymine. “guanine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The two strands of DNA are antiparallel, which means they:
items.why: It's run in opposite 5-prime to 3-prime directions. “are exactly the same length” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: DNA replication is semi-conservative because each new molecule contains:
items.correct: one original strand and one new strand
items.why: The answer examiners want is: one original strand and one new strand. “two completely new strands” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A nucleosome consists of DNA wound around:
items.why: Correct answer: a core of histone proteins. “a single RNA molecule” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A DNA sample is 30% adenine. Using Chargaff's rule, what percentage is cytosine?
items.why: Exact answer: 20 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
